Novos/TPsicoGen_Eng.pdf: Psychogenic Therapy: An Evolutionary Approach to Meme– Gene Conflicts João Carlos Holland de Barcellos Abstract This article presents Psychogenic Therapy, or the psychogenist approach, as a theoretical proposal for psychological intervention aimed at reducing conflicts between genetically based instinctive predispositions and culturally acquired belief systems. Such conflicts, termed meme–gene conflicts, arise when internalized norms and values oppose behavioral tendencies evolutionarily shaped to maximize genetic preservation, both of the individual and of relatives who share part of their genetic material, in accordance with the concept of inclusive fitness. The proposed therapeutic approach consists of identifying and reevaluating internalized memetic systems that produce persistent psychological distress, seeking greater consonance between biological predispositions and contemporary sociocultural demands. The model suggests integration between evolutionary psychology and psychotherapeutic practice, offering a theoretical framework for understanding modern psychological conflicts. Keywords: psychotherapy, evolutionary psychology, culture, memes, social adaptation, inclusive fitness. 1. Introduction Human psychological well-being results from the interaction between inherited biological factors and culturally acquired experiences throughout life. Systems of beliefs, moral norms, and social values are often internalized during childhood and adolescence, periods in which critical reasoning capacities are still developing. This process may lead individuals to adopt cultural patterns that later conflict with evolutionarily shaped instinctive behavioral predispositions. Such incompatibilities may generate persistent psychological distress, manifesting as internal tensions, anxiety, or identity conflicts. The present work proposes a conceptual model termed Psychogenic Therapy, intended to understand and mitigate conflicts arising from the interaction between biological inheritance and cultural conditioning. 2. Evolutionary Foundations of Human Behavior Evolutionary psychology proposes that many aspects of human behavior derive from adaptations shaped throughout the evolutionary history of the species (Tooby & Cosmides, 1992). These adaptations correspond to cognitive and emotional predispositions influencing preferences, motivations, and behavioral responses. Genetic preservation does not depend solely on individual reproduction. Inclusive fitness theory (Hamilton, 1964) demonstrates that genes may also propagate through the reproductive success of genetically related individuals. Consequently, behavioral tendencies such as parental care, familial cooperation, and formation of social bonds possess deep evolutionary roots. Wilson (1975) and Lumsden and Wilson (1981) suggest that such predispositions operate through epigenetic rules that guide the interaction between genetic inheritance and cultural learning. However, modern cultural contexts may introduce normative systems that come into tension with these predispositions, generating internal psychological conflicts. 3. Meme–Gene Conflicts The concept of meme, introduced by Dawkins (1976), refers to culturally transmitted units such as ideas, values, and behavioral patterns. When internalized memetic systems conflict with genetically grounded instinctive predispositions, psychological tensions may arise, here termed meme–gene conflicts. Such conflicts may occur when cultural norms repress relevant instinctive impulses or when natural impulses clash with internalized moral values, generating guilt, anxiety, or psychological distress. Additionally, certain cultural environments may discourage behaviors linked to family cooperation, formation of affective bonds, or desired reproduction, intensifying tensions between social expectations and evolutionary predispositions. Human adaptation therefore requires ongoing negotiation between cultural demands and biological tendencies. 4. Proposal of Psychogenic Therapy Psychogenic Therapy proposes that part of contemporary psychological suffering results from incompatibilities between instinctive predispositions and internalized cultural systems. The therapeutic objective consists of identifying such conflicts and assisting individuals in reorganizing priorities and beliefs in ways more compatible with their psychological and social needs. This approach does not imply biological determinism but recognizes that evolutionary predispositions influence fundamental human motivations, such as social belonging, formation of emotional bonds, and pursuit of social recognition. The proposed therapeutic process involves: • Identifying internalized beliefs and values that generate distress; • Evaluating conflicts between cultural demands and personal predispositions; • Reorganizing individual goals and expectations; • Reducing internal tensions associated with conflicts between culture and instinct. 5. Motivational Imbalances Another source of psychological suffering may arise when certain adaptive goals are pursued disproportionately at the expense of other relevant dimensions of human life. Examples include exclusive dedication to professional career advancement, excessive pursuit of economic status, extreme focus on physical appearance, or parenthood without adequate support conditions. Such imbalances may generate personal frustration and social conflicts. The psychogenist approach seeks to identify these imbalances and promote greater harmony among different dimensions of individual life. 6. Role of the Therapist Within this framework, the therapist acts as a facilitator in identifying internal conflicts between cultural values and personal predispositions, assisting in the reorganization of priorities and expectations. The objective is to promote greater consonance between social demands, individual dispositions, and personal life projects, thereby reducing persistent internal tensions. 7. Conclusion Psychogenic Therapy constitutes a theoretical proposal integrating concepts from evolutionary psychology and cultural psychology to understand psychological conflicts resulting from the interaction between biological inheritance and cultural conditioning. Although conceptual in nature, the model suggests possibilities for future empirical investigation and potential clinical applications, contributing to interdisciplinary discussions on mental health and social adaptation in contemporary societies. References Dawkins, R. (1976). The Selfish Gene. Oxford University Press. Hamilton, W. D. (1964). The genetical evolution of social behaviour I & II. Journal of Theoretical Biology, 7, 1–52. Lumsden, C. J., & Wilson, E. O. (1981). Genes, Mind, and Culture: The Coevolutionary Process. Harvard University Press. Pinker, S. (1997). How the Mind Works. W. W. Norton & Company. Tooby, J., & Cosmides, L. (1992). The psychological foundations of culture. In Barkow, J., Cosmides, L., & Tooby, J. (Eds.), The Adapted Mind. Oxford University Press. Wilson, E. O. (1975). Sociobiology: The New Synthesis. Harvard University Press.
Shorts/ChatGPT_DUT_Eng.pdf: ChatGpt: Decreasing Universe Theory (DUT) —Summary 1. Central idea The Decreasing Universe Theory (DUT) proposes that the Universe is not actually expanding. Instead, what changes over time are the physical scales of matter — particles, atoms, and characteristic lengths — which would gradually decrease. Since all our measuring devices (rulers, clocks, spectroscopic standards) are also made of the same matter, the shrinking of physical scales would make the Universe appear to expand when comparing observations from different epochs. Conceptually: Universe appears to expand Thus, observations usually interpreted as cosmic expansion would instead be interpreted as an effect of changing physical scales. 2. Motivation of the theory The DUT emerges as a conceptual alternative to cosmological models based on the metric expansion of space, such as the standard ΛCDM model. Its motivations include: • Searching for alternative explanations for the cosmological redshift. • Avoiding the need for certain elements of the standard model, such as specific interpretations of dark energy. • Exploring the possibility that fundamental physical scales or constants evolve with cosmic time. 3. Explanation of redshift In the standard cosmological model (ΛCDM), redshift is interpreted mainly as a consequence of the expansion of space between the emission and observation of light. In the DUT, redshift would instead arise from changes in physical scales: 1. Atoms in the past would be larger, or would emit photons with different intrinsic properties. 2. Over time, the scale of matter would decrease. 3. Light emitted in the past would therefore be observed today with a longer wavelength, producing redshift. Thus: without requiring expansion of space itself. 4. Theoretical implications If the DUT were correct, several interpretations in cosmology would need to be reconsidered. Proposed implications include: • Cosmological redshift reinterpreted as a scale-evolution effect. • A possible alternative explanation for phenomena currently attributed to dark energy. • A new interpretation of the relationship between stellar age and observed redshift. • The possibility that some effects attributed to dark matter could instead be observational artifacts related to scale evolution or photon propagation effects. 5. Comparison with the standard cosmological model (ΛCDM) Aspect ΛCDM (standard model) DUT Expansion of the Space expands Physical scales of matter shrink Universe Evolution of physical scales or Redshift Metric expansion of space emission properties Required to explain Dark energy May not be necessary accelerated expansion Interpretation of cosmic Universe becomes larger Physical scales become smaller evolution over time over time 6. Possible observational tests For the DUT to be considered a viable alternative, it must reproduce the large body of existing cosmological observations. Important tests include: • Type Ia supernovae (distance–redshift relations) • Cosmic Microwave Background (CMB) • Baryon Acoustic Oscillations (BAO) • Large-scale structure formation • Relationships between galaxy properties and redshift Additional tests may involve comparing galaxies with: • the same mass • the same distance measured independently of redshift • different stellar ages to investigate correlations between stellar population age and residual redshift. See also: Some Articles about D.U.T: https://jocaxx.github.io/DUniverse/ the Decreasing Universe Theory (DUT) as it is presented in the available papers and summaries by João Carlos Holland de Barcellos. Overview The Decreasing Universe Theory proposes that the main large-scale cosmological effect is not the expansion of space, but a continuous contraction of space and matter under gravity. In this picture, the Universe appears to expand because the physical scales used to measure it are themselves shrinking over time. The theory is presented as an alternative to the standard ΛCDM model and claims to address cosmic redshift, accelerated expansion, dark matter effects, and some CMB-related questions without relying on the usual interpretation of expanding space, dark energy, or dark matter. (ResearchGate) Core postulate The central postulate is that the gravitational field continuously contracts space and everything embedded in it. That means not only astronomical structures but also local physical scales may shrink with time. In the theory’s own framing, this contraction is cumulative and universal, though stronger effects are often associated with stronger gravitational environments. (CME Live) Conceptually, DUT replaces this standard idea: with this alternative: How DUT explains cosmological redshift In standard cosmology, a distant galaxy’s light is redshifted because space stretches while the photon travels. In DUT, the redshift is instead interpreted as an effect of relative scale change between emission and observation. The wavelength we measure today looks longer not because space expanded, but because the measuring framework and/or the emitting system changed scale over cosmic time. The theory’s summaries explicitly present DUT as an alternative explanation for cosmological redshift. (CME Live) In practical terms, DUT says the Universe may look as if distant galaxies are receding faster and faster, while the deeper mechanism is contraction of space/matter rather than expansion of space itself. (CME Live) Claimed explanation of apparent accelerated expansion One of the theory’s recurring claims is that it can explain the apparent accelerated expansion of galaxies without dark energy. In the DUT framing, acceleration is not a genuine acceleration of the scale factor of the Universe; it is an observational consequence of contraction-driven scale changes. The theory therefore tries to replace the dark-energy interpretation with a contraction-based one. (CME Live) Claimed explanation of dark matter effects A second major claim is that DUT can reproduce galaxy rotation effects normally attributed to dark matter. One paper explicitly presents the “dark matter effect” as an apparent consequence of the DUT mechanism, arguing that contraction and photon- related effects can mimic the observed rotation-curve anomaly without requiring dark matter as a separate substance. (PhilPapers) That is one of the theory’s strongest advertised ambitions: a single mechanism that would reinterpret both • apparent cosmic acceleration, and • anomalous galactic dynamics. (CME Live) Role of gravity in DUT Gravity is not just an attractive force in the usual sense. In DUT, gravity is elevated into a more global geometric driver of contraction. The theory’s descriptions say that gravitational fields induce continuous spatial contraction, and that more massive systems should be associated with stronger contraction effects. (independent.academia.edu) This point matters because it leads directly to one of DUT’s main observational predictions. Main observational prediction: mass vs. redshift at fixed distance A repeated DUT prediction is the following: For galaxies at the same distance, more massive galaxies should show smaller redshifts on average. This is presented as the opposite of what the author argues the standard model would imply from ordinary gravitational redshift considerations. Several DUT papers frame this as a falsifiable test: compare galaxies at the same independently measured distance and see whether higher mass is associated with lower observed redshift. (PhilPapers) That prediction is important because it turns DUT from a purely verbal reinterpretation into something testable against galaxy data. Extension of the theory to stellar age A related deduction, stated in summaries around the theory, is that a galaxy’s time under gravitational contraction, meaning roughly its age or evolutionary maturity, could influence the observed redshift. In that interpretation, stellar age is not merely correlated with redshift through ordinary cosmic evolution; it becomes part of the DUT mechanism itself. This is why age–redshift tests are natural within the DUT framework. (independent.academia.edu) DUT and the CMB The theory has also been extended to the Cosmic Microwave Background and the age of the Universe. One paper explicitly says it uses DUT to estimate the age of the Universe from the average wavelength of the CMB radiation. That means DUT is not only trying to reinterpret galaxy redshift; it is also trying to offer an alternative account of one of the central pillars of modern cosmology. (PhilArchive) Philosophical structure of the theory At a conceptual level, DUT is a scale reinterpretation theory. Instead of saying “the cosmos gets bigger,” it says “the standard of measurement gets smaller.” This is why the theory often sounds counterintuitive: many observational signatures of expansion are preserved, but the ontological explanation is reversed. So, in DUT: • the Universe need not be expanding in the standard sense, • redshift need not imply expanding space, • dark energy may be unnecessary, • dark matter may be unnecessary or partly illusory, • gravitational contraction becomes the unifying mechanism. (CME Live) What DUT would have to explain to become competitive This is where the theory faces its real burden. A viable cosmological theory cannot explain only one or two effects. To compete with ΛCDM, DUT would need to reproduce, in a quantitatively consistent way: • the Type Ia supernova distance–redshift relation, • the detailed angular power spectrum of the CMB, • baryon acoustic oscillations, • large-scale structure formation, • gravitational lensing observations, • primordial nucleosynthesis constraints, • local and high-redshift galaxy evolution data. The publicly available DUT texts clearly attempt alternative explanations for some major effects, but from the standpoint of mainstream cosmology, the central question is whether the full observational package can be reproduced with comparable precision. The available sources present DUT as an alternative proposal, not as a theory that has already replaced ΛCDM in the literature. (ResearchGate) Scientific status As of the currently available sources, DUT appears as a non-mainstream, author-driven alternative cosmological proposal, with papers and summaries on repositories and journals outside the core consensus literature. The sources found include PhilArchive, ResearchGate summaries, vixra/MedCrave-style publications, and related postings. That means the theory exists in written form and makes explicit claims, but it is not part of the standard accepted cosmological framework. (PhilArchive) Clean summary of DUT in one paragraph The Decreasing Universe Theory says that the Universe may only appear to expand because gravity causes a long-term contraction of space and matter, changing the physical scale of atoms, objects, and measurements over time. Under this interpretation, cosmological redshift is not primarily due to expanding space, apparent acceleration does not require dark energy, and some galaxy-dynamics anomalies may not require dark matter. The theory’s signature empirical claim is that, at equal distance, more massive galaxies should tend to exhibit smaller redshifts, with possible related effects involving galaxy age. (PhilPapers) Bottom line DUT is best understood as a global contraction cosmology: a theory that tries to reinterpret the main observational evidence for expansion as evidence for shrinking physical scales instead. Its appeal is conceptual unification. Its weakness, at present, is the very high evidential bar required to replace ΛCDM. If you want, I can turn this into a more formal essay-style exposition, with sections like Foundations, Mechanism, Predictions, Comparison with ΛCDM, and Main Objections.
Shorts/DeepSeek_DUT_Eng.pdf: DeepSeek: Here is the comprehensive summary of "Decreasing Universe" theory. A Comprehensive Summary of the "Decreasing Universe" Theory (Jocax Barcellos) This is a comprehensive summary of the "Decreasing Universe" (Universo Decrescente) theory proposed by Jocax Barcellos (João Carlos Holland de Barcellos), based on his available articles and other sources. It is an alternative cosmological model that seeks to explain phenomena such as the accelerated expansion of the universe and the rotation curves of galaxies without resorting to the concepts of Dark Energy and Dark Matter, which are pillars of the standard cosmological model (ΛCDM) . The Fundamental Postulate: Gravitational Contraction of Space The core of the theory is the hypothesis that the gravitational field induces a continuous contraction of space . This means that regions subject to a significant gravitational field, such as the vicinity of a planet, star, or galaxy, are constantly contracting. A crucial point is that this contraction is imperceptible to a local observer (called the "Local Observer" - LO), because everything within that region contracts at the same rate: the atoms, the measuring rulers, the observer themselves. To the LO, their one- meter ruler remains exactly one meter long . To formalize this idea, the theory defines some concepts and a fundamental mathematical entity: • Local Space (LS): The region under non-negligible gravitational influence, which undergoes contraction . • Outer Space (OS): Intergalactic space, with a weak gravitational field, which does not undergo contraction . • Sideral Observer (SO): A hypothetical observer located in "Outer Space" who could perceive the contraction of our "Local Space" over time . • Jocaxian Time (T<sub>j</sub>): The period of time required for "Local Space" (and everything within it) to contract to half its original size, from the perspective of a "Sideral Observer" . • Jocaxian Factor (F<sub>j</sub>): The mathematical expression describing this contraction over time. The proposed formula is: Fj(Δt) = 2^(Δt / Tj) . Explanation for "Dark Energy" (The Apparent Accelerated Expansion) How does this local contraction explain the observation that distant galaxies appear to be moving away at an accelerated rate? The reasoning is as follows : 1. Intergalactic space (OS) does not contract. 2. Our local space (LS), here on Earth, contracts continuously (from the SO's point of view). 3. Our measuring rulers, therefore, are shrinking over time. 4. When we measure the distance to a distant galaxy (which is in the OS), we use our rulers, which are getting smaller. A distant object, whose position hasn't changed in the OS, will appear progressively farther away simply because our measuring instrument (the ruler) has shrunk. Thus, the impression that galaxies are moving away is, according to the theory, an optical illusion caused by the contraction of our own reference frame. The formula for the apparent distance (D) of a galaxy, as a function of time, would be: D(Δt) = D0 * Fj(Δt), where D0 is the distance at the moment the light was emitted . Explanation for "Dark Matter" (The Effect on Rotation Curves) Another pillar of the model is the explanation for the rotation speed of stars at the edges of galaxies, which is higher than predicted by visible matter alone . The Decreasing Universe theory proposes that this effect is also a consequence of local space contraction. The contraction would alter the perceived dynamics, creating the impression that there is extra mass ("dark matter") pulling on the stars. The author derives a formula that, he claims, reproduces the velocity curve observed in galaxies like M33 (Messier 33) . Predictions and Tests One of the scientific aspects of the theory is that it makes testable predictions, which differentiate it from the ΛCDM model. The main one is : • Decreasing Universe Prediction: For galaxies at the same distance, those with greater mass should exhibit a smaller redshift on average. This is because they would experience the local contraction effect more intensely. • Standard Model Prediction (ΛCDM): For galaxies at the same distance, those with greater mass should have a slightly larger redshift, due to the energy loss of photons escaping their more intense gravitational field. Summary of Key Concepts To make it easier to visualize, the table below summarizes the main aspects of the theory: Concept Description Formula/Entity Root Cause Space contraction induced by gravity. Gravitational Fie Does not perceive the contraction, as everything around Local Observer (LO) Earth and its inst them contracts along with them. Would perceive the contraction of local space over Sideral Observer (SO) Hypothetical time. Mathematical Entity Factor that quantifies contraction over time. Fj(Δt) = 2^(Δt / T Explanation for Dark Illusion of recession caused by the contraction of our D(Δt) = D0 * Fj(Δ Energy own rulers. Explanation for Dark Dynamic effect caused by the contraction of local space Reproduction of Matter within galaxies. velocity curve See also: Some articles about D.U.T.: https://jocaxx.github.io/DUniverse/
Shorts/DUT_AI_1_ENG.pdf: The Hypothesis of Continuous Gravitational Contraction as a Foundation for the Decreasing Universe Theory: Redshift, Cosmological Time, Rotation Curves, and Observational Tests Abstract This article presents a synthetic formulation of the Decreasing Universe Theory — DUT — based on the propositions, equations, data, and observational tests contained in the documents analyzed. The theory's central hypothesis is that the gravitational field causes a continuous contraction of space and of all objects within it, including measuring instruments. This contraction would be extremely slow on human scales but cumulative on cosmological scales, potentially producing observational effects normally interpreted as accelerated expansion of the universe, dark energy, dark matter, cosmological redshift, and time dilation. From this hypothesis, the documents derive relationships between apparent distance, comoving distance, and redshift; propose an explanation for the time dilation observed in Type Ia supernovae; formulate an alternative for galactic rotation curves; derive the Tully-Fisher relation; and propose observational tests involving galactic mass, morphological age, and redshift. The results presented in the appendices indicate that, in some datasets, more massive or older galaxies would tend to exhibit lower redshift under comparable conditions. However, the documents themselves also indicate relevant limitations: small sample size, reliance on stellar mass as an approximation of total mass, projection effects, peculiar velocities, and the need for greater mathematical formalization. It is concluded that DUT, as analyzed here, constitutes an alternative theoretical program that is internally coherent in its unifying ambition, but still dependent on independent empirical validation and rigorous quantitative comparison with competing cosmological models. Keywords: Decreasing Universe Theory; redshift; gravitational contraction; dark energy; dark matter; Tully-Fisher; Type Ia supernovae; alternative cosmology. 1. Introduction The Decreasing Universe Theory — DUT — starts from a simple physical proposition: gravitational fields would not merely curve or affect space, but would cause a continuous contraction of space and of everything within it. In the documents analyzed, this contraction includes material objects, atomic standards, and measuring instruments. Thus, an observer situated within a gravitational field would not locally perceive their own contraction, since their rulers, clocks, and physical standards would contract along with them. The difference would become observable only in cosmological comparisons between regions subjected to distinct gravitational histories. In the appendices, DUT is presented as an interpretive alternative to phenomena that, in the standard cosmological model, are usually explained by cosmic expansion, dark energy, dark matter, peculiar velocities, or relativistic effects. The theory proposes that the apparent accelerated recession of galaxies could result from the progressive contraction of local distance standards. If terrestrial instruments gradually become smaller, extragalactic distances would be measured as progressively larger, producing the impression of accelerated recession. The same hypothesis is extended to redshift. The longer a photon's travel time to Earth, the longer the period during which the observer and their instruments would have continued to contract; therefore, the received wavelength would be measured as relatively longer. Redshift, therefore, would not depend only on distance, but also on the mass of the emitting galaxy, the galaxy's age, and the peculiar dynamics of the observed system. This article organizes the propositions from the attached documents into scientific format, with emphasis on four elements: hypothesis, methodology, theoretical model / calculation, and conclusion. The goal is not to assert the definitive validation of DUT, but to reconstruct its internal logic and indicate how its data and equations are mobilized to support an alternative cosmological hypothesis. 2. Hypothesis The general hypothesis can be formulated in the following terms: Main hypothesis: The gravitational field causes a continuous contraction of space, matter, and local measurement standards. This contraction is cumulative over time and proportional to the intensity of the gravitational field. On cosmological scales, this effect could produce redshift, time dilation, an apparent increase in distances, anomalous galactic rotation curves, and empirical relations usually attributed to the expansion of the universe, dark energy, or dark matter. This hypothesis unfolds into four specific propositions. The first proposition is that the apparent distance of extragalactic objects increases because local measurement standards decrease. In the documents, this relationship is expressed by a formula in which the proper or apparent distance depends on the comoving distance and an exponential factor associated with the Hubble constant and the time interval: D(Δt) = D₀ · e^(H₀Δt) In this formulation, D is the apparent distance measured by a terrestrial observer, D₀ is the comoving distance, and Δt is the time interval elapsed from the photon's emission to its reception. The second proposition is that redshift can be interpreted as the cumulative effect of the local contraction of measurement standards. The article on distance as a function of redshift derives the relation: 1 + z = e^(H₀T) and, from it: T = ln(1 + z) / H₀ Since T = D₀/c, the comoving distance as a function of redshift is presented as: D₀ = c·ln(1 + z) / H₀ and the proper or apparent distance as: D = c(1 + z)·ln(1 + z) / H₀ These expressions constitute one of the mathematical bases of DUT for reinterpreting the relationship between redshift and cosmological distance. The third proposition is that galactic mass and age affect redshift. In the appendices, the theory predicts that, holding distance and age constant, more massive galaxies should exhibit lower redshift, since greater gravitational intensity would produce greater contraction and, therefore, a shorter emitted wavelength. Similarly, holding mass and distance constant, older galaxies should exhibit lower redshift, since they would have accumulated more time of gravitational contraction. The fourth proposition is that effects attributed to dark matter could be reinterpreted as effects of differential gravitational contraction. According to the documents, stars in regions farther from the galactic center are subjected to less intense gravitational fields, which would produce less contraction and a longer relative wavelength. This effect would be measured as greater redshift and interpreted, in the conventional model, as greater orbital velocity. 3. Methodology The methodology adopted in the documents can be reconstructed on three levels: theoretical-deductive, mathematical, and observational. 3.1. Theoretical-deductive procedure The first level consists of taking the single hypothesis of continuous gravitational contraction as a starting point. From it, the documents seek to deduce different phenomena: Hubble's Law, the distance-redshift relation, cosmological time dilation, galactic rotation curves, the Tully-Fisher relation, and correlations between mass, age, and redshift. This strategy seeks to show that a single premise could explain phenomena usually attributed to distinct mechanisms. 3.2. Mathematical procedure The second level consists of formalization through exponential functions. Contraction is modeled by factors of the type: Fⱼ(Δt) = e^(H₀Δt) or, in more general formulations, by factors dependent on mass (M), radial distance (R), and contraction time. In the article on the Tully-Fisher relation, for example, contraction is generalized with the additional hypothesis that the contraction time is inversely proportional to the intensity of the gravitational field. From this hypothesis, a factor dependent on M/R² is introduced. The article on time dilation applies similar reasoning to units of time. If the unit of distance in a proper reference frame contracts as: mₜ = m₀ / e^(H₀Δt) then the unit of time is also altered. From the physical definition of the second and the comparison between comoving and proper reference frames, the document derives: Sₜ = S ₀ / Z and, for the numerical measure of time: Tₜ = Z·T₀ This relation is used to explain the cosmological time dilation observed in Type Ia supernovae. 3.3. Observational procedure The third level is observational. The documents present three main types of tests. The first test compares pairs of galaxies with similar approximate distance, different mass, and redshift greater than 0.01. The prediction is simple: at the same distance, the more massive galaxy should exhibit lower redshift. The document reports that 47 of 50 pairs, or 94%, obey this condition. The second test uses galactic morphology as an approximation for age. Elliptical and lenticular galaxies are treated as older populations; spiral galaxies, as younger populations. In clusters such as Virgo, Coma, and Centaurus, the documents present average redshift differences in which elliptical/lenticular galaxies exhibit lower redshift than spiral galaxies. The third test seeks to isolate variables within subgroups of the Coma Cluster. The sample is restricted to 20 elliptical/lenticular galaxies, with the goal of keeping distance and age approximately constant. The set is then divided into subgroups with a redshift variation of about 150 km/s, seeking to reduce the effect of peculiar velocities. DUT's prediction is a negative correlation between stellar mass and observed redshift. 4. Theoretical Model and Main Calculations 4.1. Distance, redshift, and photon travel time The model starts from the relation: D(Δt) = D₀·e^(H₀Δt) This equation expresses the idea that the apparent distance increases because the local measurement standard decreases. Applied to wavelength, the same logic leads to: λ = λ₀·e^(H₀T) The definition of redshift is: z = (λ − λ₀) / λ₀ Hence: 1 + z = e^(H₀T) and: T = ln(1 + z) / H₀ Since D₀ = cT, one obtains: D₀ = c·ln(1 + z) / H₀ This equation is presented as the direct formula for comoving distance as a function of redshift. The apparent distance, in turn, is given by: D = D₀(1 + z) therefore: D = c(1 + z)·ln(1 + z) / H₀ The central point is that, in DUT, redshift is treated not merely as an effect of spatial expansion, but as a consequence of the difference between contracted measurement standards and photons that traverse regions of negligible gravity over long time intervals. 4.2. Cosmological time dilation The temporal application of DUT starts from the idea that the physical definition of the second depends on atomic standards, and that these standards would also be affected by gravitational contraction. The document on Type Ia supernovae distinguishes between comoving time (S₀) and proper time (Sₜ). If the contraction factor is: Z = e^(H₀Δt) then the proper unit of time is: Sₜ = S ₀ / Z Consequently, the numerical measure of a physical duration in the proper reference frame becomes: Tₜ = Z·T₀ This equation is used to interpret the cosmological time dilation of Type Ia supernovae. The article states that the observed duration of SN1A explosions grows by the factor associated with (1 + z), which would be consistent with DUT's prediction. 4.3. Mass, age, and redshift In the documents, redshift depends on at least three factors: distance, mass, and age. Distance increases redshift because it extends the photon's travel time; mass reduces redshift when the emitting galaxy is more massive, since greater gravity would imply greater contraction and a shorter emitted wavelength; age reduces redshift because older galaxies would have accumulated more contraction over time. This proposition leads to two observational predictions: M₁ > M₂ ⟹ z₁ < z₂ when the galaxies are at the same distance and have comparable age; and: Age₁ > Age₂ ⟹ z₁ < z₂ when mass and distance are approximately controlled. In the galaxy pairs presented in the appendices, 47 of 50 cases are classified as favorable to the prediction “greater mass, lower redshift.” In the morphology-based test, the averages presented are: Young type — Cluster Old type — E/S0 Difference Spiral Virgo 950 km/s 1450 km/s +500 km/s Coma 6900 km/s 7100 km/s +200 km/s Centaurus 3000 km/s 3500 km/s +500 km/s The proposed interpretation is that elliptical/lenticular galaxies, being older, would have accumulated greater contraction and would therefore exhibit lower average redshift. The document itself acknowledges, however, that the ΛCDM model offers an alternative explanation based on morphological segregation and peculiar velocities within clusters. 4.4. Galactic rotation curves To explain rotation curves without dark matter, the documents propose that stars in different regions of a galaxy emit photons subjected to different contraction histories. The Newtonian orbital velocity is expressed as: V = √(MG/R) but the apparent velocity measured on Earth would also include a differential contraction factor. The general equation presented for the apparent rotation curve is: Vₜ = c · [ ( (1/c)·√(MG/R) + 1 ) · e^( H₀T₀ − J₀MT₀/R² ) − 1 ] In this expression, c is the speed of light, M is the mass of the emitting galaxy, G is the gravitational constant, R is the distance from the star to the galactic center, H₀ is the Hubble constant, T₀ is the contraction time, and J₀ is the Jocaxian constant. The document applies this equation to the galaxy Messier 33 and states that the resulting curve reproduces the behavior traditionally attributed to dark matter. 4.5. Tully-Fisher relation The article on the Tully-Fisher relation starts from the empirical relation: M ∝ V⁴ According to the document, DUT allows this relation to be derived from the galactic rotation equation and from two astrophysical approximations for spiral galaxies: an approximately constant mass-to-light ratio and an approximately uniform central surface brightness, associated with Freeman's Law. Assuming that the mass of a spiral galaxy is proportional to the area of its disk: M = ρ·π·R² one obtains: R = √(M / (π·ρ)) Substituting this relation into the Newtonian form of velocity: V = √(MG/R) one obtains, in proportional terms: V ∝ M^(1/4) hence: M ∝ V⁴ The document concludes that the Tully-Fisher relation could be derived without recourse to dark matter, provided the approximations regarding surface density and mass-to-light ratio for spiral galaxies are accepted. 5. Results and Discussion The results presented in the documents can be grouped into four sets. The first set concerns the distance-redshift relation. DUT proposes direct formulas for comoving distance and apparent distance as functions of redshift. The strength of this formulation lies in its mathematical simplicity: the relation depends on c, H₀, and ln(1 + z). The limitation lies in the fact that the document itself acknowledges that the formula would be more adequate for galaxies of mass similar to the Milky Way, and could be compromised when applied to much more or much less massive galaxies. The second set concerns cosmological time dilation. The theory derives the relation Tₜ = Z·T₀, applying it to the observed duration of Type Ia supernovae. The argument is consistent with DUT's internal logic, since time and space are treated as physical standards equally affected by gravitational contraction. However, the demonstration requires broader quantitative comparison with observational databases independent of SN1A light curves. The third set concerns galactic mass and age as redshift factors. The tests presented are favorable to DUT in some cases: 47 of 50 galaxy pairs obey the condition “greater mass, lower redshift”; elliptical/lenticular galaxies exhibit lower average redshift than spirals in Virgo, Coma, and Centaurus; and subgroups of the Coma Cluster show negative correlations between stellar mass and redshift in two of the four groups analyzed. The fourth set concerns galactic dynamics. DUT proposes that rotation curves and the Tully-Fisher relation can be derived without dark matter. The equation applied to Messier 33 and the derivation of M ∝ V⁴ constitute attempts to show that the theory reproduces important empirical regularities. The main limitation is that these derivations depend on auxiliary hypotheses — especially regarding surface density, mass-to-light ratio, and simultaneous galaxy formation — that need to be tested on larger samples. The most methodologically careful test is that of the Coma subgroups. In it, the documents seek to control for distance, age, and radial velocity. The results, however, are mixed: subgroup 2 shows a correlation of −0.8477; subgroup 4 shows a correlation of −0.6789; but subgroup 1 shows a perfect positive correlation, albeit with only two galaxies, and subgroup 3 shows a weak positive correlation. The document itself acknowledges that the absence of a universal negative correlation indicates that the effect proposed by DUT is not the only factor involved. The limitations made explicit are relevant: small subgroups, the use of stellar mass as an approximation of total mass, possible projection effects, and differences in depth within the cluster. For this reason, the most appropriate conclusion is not that DUT has been confirmed, but that its preliminary results justify further investigation and more robust mathematical formalization. 6. Conclusion Based on the documents analyzed, the Decreasing Universe Theory can be formulated as a theoretical program based on a central hypothesis: gravity produces continuous contraction of space, matter, and local measurement standards. From this hypothesis, the texts construct alternative explanations for cosmological redshift, apparent distance, time dilation, galactic rotation curves, the Tully-Fisher relation, and correlations between mass, age, and redshift. DUT's main contribution lies in its unifying ambition. Rather than separately invoking dark energy, dark matter, accelerated expansion, and local dynamical effects, the theory seeks to derive multiple phenomena from a single premise. Its fundamental equations — especially D = D₀e^(H₀Δt), 1 + z = e^(H₀T), D₀ = c·ln(1 + z)/H₀, and Tₜ = Z·T₀ — mathematically organize this proposal. The data presented in the appendices are compatible with some of the theory's predictions: more massive galaxies appear, in several pairs, with lower redshift; morphologically older galaxies exhibit lower average redshift in some clusters; and Coma subgroups show negative correlations between mass and redshift when certain variables are controlled. In addition, the documents propose an equation for rotation curves and a derivation of the Tully-Fisher relation. However, DUT's scientific validity still depends on important advances. It is necessary to more fully formalize the relationship between gravitational field, mass, exposure time, and contraction. It is also essential to test the predictions on independent astronomical catalogs, with larger samples, homogeneous total-mass estimates, statistical control of peculiar velocities, and direct quantitative comparison with the ΛCDM model. Thus, the most balanced conclusion is that DUT, as presented in the attached documents, constitutes an alternative cosmological hypothesis that is coherent in its internal formulation and endowed with testable predictions, but not yet sufficiently demonstrated to replace the standard cosmological model. Its immediate academic value lies in proposing a research agenda: transforming the hypothesis of continuous gravitational contraction into a fully specified mathematical model and submitting it to independent, replicable, and statistically robust observational tests.
Shorts/DUT_AI_2_ENG.pdf: The Decreasing Universe Theory as an Alternative Program for Cosmological Interpretation: Redshift, Time, Apparent Dark Matter, and Observational Tests Abstract This article critically reviews a set of papers devoted to the so-called Decreasing Universe Theory — DUT —, a proposal according to which gravitational fields produce a continuous contraction of space, of objects, and of the measuring instruments located within it. The review aims to analyze the internal coherence of this theoretical program and to examine how its papers mobilize a common hypothesis to reinterpret phenomena usually associated with cosmic expansion, dark energy, dark matter, cosmological redshift, the time dilation observed in Type Ia supernovae, the wavelength of the cosmic microwave background, and observational correlations involving mass, galactic age, and redshift. The corpus analyzed includes theoretical papers, cosmological applications, and preliminary empirical tests. The analysis shows that DUT exhibits a strong unifying ambition: it seeks to derive, from a single premise, effects attributed in the standard cosmological model to different entities or mechanisms. At the same time, the documents themselves reveal important limitations, especially the need for greater mathematical formalization of the relationship between contraction, mass, gravitational field, and time, as well as the need for independent tests on large samples, with rigorous control of peculiar velocities, projection effects, total mass, and stellar age. It is concluded that DUT, as presented in the documents analyzed, should be understood as a speculative yet testable theoretical program, whose main contribution is to formulate alternative observational hypotheses, but whose scientific acceptance would depend on independent empirical validation and systematic quantitative comparison with competing models. Keywords: Decreasing Universe Theory; redshift; dark matter; dark energy; Tully-Fisher relation; Type Ia supernovae; alternative cosmology. 1. Introduction Contemporary cosmology is structured, to a large extent, around the ΛCDM model, which combines cosmic expansion, the cosmological constant or dark energy, cold dark matter, and general relativity to explain a wide range of observations. The documents analyzed here propose an alternative interpretation, called the Decreasing Universe Theory — DUT —, whose central principle is that the gravitational field continuously contracts space and everything within it. According to this hypothesis, the contraction would be extremely slow on local scales but cumulative on cosmological scales, potentially becoming perceptible in long-duration astronomical phenomena. In the article on time dilation in Type Ia supernovae, for example, it is stated that contraction within Earth's gravitational field would be on the order of 7% per billion years, and that observers located in regions of negligible gravity could be treated as “comoving” observers, while observers immersed in gravitational fields would be “proper” observers. DUT's proposal shifts the interpretive focus: what appears as accelerated expansion of the universe could result from the contraction of local measurement standards; what appears as cosmological redshift could be partly an effect of comparing physical systems subjected to different contraction histories; and what appears as anomalous orbital velocity in galaxies could be reinterpreted as a consequence of differential gravitational contraction. The article on Type Ia supernovae itself lists three factors that would contribute to galactic redshift under DUT: mass, age, and distance. More massive galaxies would tend to exhibit lower redshift under comparable conditions; older galaxies would also tend to exhibit lower redshift; and more distant objects would tend to exhibit higher redshift due to the longer photon propagation time to the observer. The purpose of this article is to critically analyze, in Mini Review format, how the set of attached documents constructs this hypothesis and applies it to different cosmological and galactic problems. The analysis does not aim to demonstrate the external validity of DUT or to replace a systematic review of the international cosmological literature. Its scope is more limited: to reconstruct the program's internal logic, identify its main applications, examine the proposed observational tests, and point out methodological and theoretical gaps that would need to be overcome for the theory to be evaluated according to more demanding academic standards. The review is organized into three main sections. The first examines the foundations of gravitational contraction and its applications to redshift, distance, cosmological time, Type Ia supernovae, and the cosmic microwave background. The second discusses applications to galactic dynamics, especially rotation curves and the Tully-Fisher relation. The third analyzes the observational tests involving mass, galactic age, and redshift, highlighting favorable results, mixed results, and methodological limitations. 2. Review Methodology This work adopts a critical narrative review methodology, appropriate when the goal is not to perform a statistical meta-analysis but to conceptually organize a delimited set of texts. The documents attached by the user were analyzed, all centered on the Decreasing Universe Theory or its applications. The corpus includes papers on distance-redshift, time dilation in Type Ia supernovae, the cosmic microwave background and the age of the universe, the apparent dark matter effect, the Tully-Fisher relation, mass-redshift correlation, galactic age and redshift, as well as tests on subgroups of galaxies in the Coma Cluster. The reading was guided by four questions: first, what is DUT's central hypothesis and how is it mathematically formulated? Second, how is this hypothesis applied to cosmological phenomena usually explained by expansion, dark energy, or relativistic effects? Third, how does the theory seek to reinterpret phenomena associated with dark matter and galactic dynamics? Fourth, what observational tests are proposed, and what limitations do the documents themselves acknowledge? Because this is a review based exclusively on the documents provided, statements about DUT are presented as internal formulations of the corpus analyzed. When the texts claim to challenge or refute the ΛCDM model, this claim is treated as the documents' position, not as a conclusion established by the scientific community. This distinction is methodologically important because the texts themselves acknowledge, at certain points, the existence of alternative explanations compatible with ΛCDM, especially in the case of redshift differences by galactic morphology and the effects of peculiar velocities in clusters. 3. Foundations of Gravitational Contraction: Distance, Redshift, and Cosmological Time DUT's core hypothesis is that the gravitational field continuously contracts space and the objects contained within it. In epistemological terms, this shifts the cosmological problem to the relationship between observer, measuring instrument, and local gravitational history. An observer immersed in a gravitational field would not directly perceive the contraction of their own measurement standards, because their instruments, their atoms, and local objects would shrink together. The effect would become perceptible only in comparison with regions or phenomena whose gravitational history was distinct. In the article on Type Ia supernovae, this distinction is expressed through the concepts of the comoving observer, located in a region of negligible gravity, and the proper observer, situated in a gravitational environment. This distinction between reference frames allows redshift to be reinterpreted. Rather than being understood exclusively as a product of the expansion of space or of recession velocities, it would, within DUT's framework, also be an effect of the contraction of local measurement standards. The more distant the observed galaxy, the longer the photon's travel time; during that interval, the observer and their instruments would continue to contract, such that the received wavelength would be measured as relatively longer. The appearance of accelerated expansion would arise, in this interpretation, from the continuous contraction of local distance standards. The article on distance as a function of redshift develops this intuition into a mathematical formulation, seeking to derive a relationship between apparent distance, comoving distance, and redshift. In later documents, this structure is taken up again in the form of the so-called Jocaxian factor, associated with an exponential function of time and, in certain contexts, with the Hubble parameter. The article on the Tully-Fisher relation, in reconstructing DUT's foundations, presents the formula for distance as measured by a contracting reference frame and links it to Hubble's law by differentiation, producing a relationship between apparent velocity and distance. DUT also seeks to extend its hypothesis to the problem of time. The article on time dilation in Type Ia supernovae argues that, if units of distance contract, units of time must also be affected, since the physical definition of the second depends on atomic processes. From the comparison between units of time in comoving and proper reference frames, the text derives a relation in which the numerical value of time measured in the proper reference frame grows by a factor associated with contraction. According to the article, this formulation would explain the cosmological time dilation observed in Type Ia supernovae, whose apparent duration increases by the factor related to redshift. The application to the CMB represents an even more ambitious extension. The article on the cosmic microwave background and the age of the universe uses DUT to estimate the cosmic age by comparing the observed average wavelength of the CMB with a wavelength that would be produced today in a laboratory by processes similar to those of a plasma at around 3000 K. The text calculates a laboratory wavelength on the order of 5 × 10⁻⁶ m, compares it with the observed average CMB wavelength of approximately 1.44 × 10⁻³ m, and obtains an estimate of 82 billion years for the age of the universe within DUT's framework. This estimate, however, should be interpreted with caution. The article itself acknowledges that the calculation assumes a contraction history associated with a gravitational field similar to the present one, even though the galaxy did not exist in its current configuration from the outset. For this reason, the text states that the calculated age should be understood as a lower bound, not as a definitive value. It is also noted that the article does not necessarily reject the possibility of a Big Bang, but redefines its status, treating the origin of the universe as an open question within the model. The main strength of this first dimension of DUT lies in its attempt to unify distance, redshift, and time under a single physical hypothesis. Its main weakness, however, lies in the need to more rigorously formalize the relationship between contraction, gravitational field intensity, mass, exposure time, and cosmological evolution. Without this formalization, the theory remains conceptually suggestive but still insufficiently determined for broad quantitative comparison with the standard model. 4. Galactic Dynamics: Rotation Curves, Apparent Dark Matter, and the Tully-Fisher Relation The second front of DUT's development concerns galactic dynamics. The article Decreasing Universe: The ‘Dark Matter’ Effect states that the gravitational contraction hypothesis could explain two phenomena usually treated separately: apparent accelerated expansion, without invoking dark energy, and anomalous orbital velocities of stars in galaxies, without invoking dark matter. The central argument is that differential gravitational contraction, varying with the intensity of the gravitational field in different regions of the galaxy, would alter the wavelength of photons emitted by stars located at different distances from the galactic center. Since stellar velocities are inferred from redshift, this difference could be mistakenly interpreted as a higher orbital velocity. In the case of rotation curves, the article takes the galaxy Messier 33 as an example. The text states that the anomalous velocity of stars and gas in the outer regions of galaxies is traditionally explained by dark matter, since the apparent velocity does not decrease as expected under a simple Newtonian reading. DUT proposes that this behavior could be reconstructed by considering that photons emitted in different regions of the galaxy would undergo distinct contraction effects before being observed. The article develops an equation for apparent velocity as a function of the galaxy's mass, the star's radial distance from the center, the gravitational constant, the Hubble constant, and the Jocaxian factor. The formulation culminates in an expression for the apparent galactic rotation curve, according to which the velocity inferred on Earth would result not only from the actual orbital velocity but also from the difference between the contraction of the emitting galaxy and the contraction of the Milky Way. The text states that this equation reproduces the observed rotation curve of Messier 33, traditionally attributed to dark matter. From the standpoint of prudent academic writing, it is important to avoid the strong claim that dark matter has been eliminated. The more rigorous formulation is to say that, in the documents analyzed, DUT proposes an alternative interpretation for observables normally associated with dark matter. This distinction is essential because dark matter, in the standard cosmological model, is inferred not only from galactic rotation curves but from multiple classes of observations. Since the documents provided focus mainly on rotation curves, Tully-Fisher, and mass-redshift correlations, the conclusion should remain restricted to that scope. The article on the Tully-Fisher relation represents an attempt to generalize DUT's application beyond a specific galactic case. The Tully-Fisher relation is presented in the document as an empirical law according to which the mass or luminosity of a spiral galaxy is proportional to the fourth power of its rotational velocity, M ∝ V⁴. The article claims to derive this relation from DUT, using the galactic rotation equation proposed in the article on the dark matter effect, together with two astrophysical approximations: an approximately constant mass-to-light ratio and Freeman's Law, understood as approximate uniformity of central surface brightness in spiral galaxies. The derivation of the Tully-Fisher relation, as presented in the document, depends on expressing the galaxy's mass as a function of its area and an approximately constant average surface density. From this approximation, the article rewrites the rotation equation in terms of surface density and arrives at the proportionality M ∝ V⁴. In its conclusion, the text states that the Tully-Fisher relation can be derived without recourse to dark matter or an exotic component, and that this would reinforce DUT's ability to reproduce empirical regularities normally attributed to the standard model. This section of the DUT program is theoretically relevant because it attempts to show that the contraction hypothesis not only explains an isolated phenomenon but can reproduce a general empirical relation. However, precisely because it depends on auxiliary hypotheses about spiral galaxies, it requires additional tests. It would be necessary to evaluate the derivation on low-surface-brightness galaxies, dwarf galaxies, systems with non-homogeneous mass distributions, galaxies with different evolutionary histories, and independent samples. Without these tests, the derivation remains interesting as a theoretical exercise but not yet sufficient to replace competing explanations. 5. Observational Tests: Mass, Galactic Age, and Redshift The third dimension of the documents analyzed is the attempt to turn DUT into an empirically testable theory. This step is crucial, since an alternative cosmological theory can only be scientifically evaluated if it produces observational predictions distinguishable from those of competing models. The most recurrent prediction in the documents is the following: under comparable distance conditions, more massive galaxies should exhibit lower redshift; and, under comparable mass and distance conditions, older galaxies should also exhibit lower redshift. The article Decreasing Universe: Redshifts and Distance Data Refute the L-CDM Model explicitly formulates this prediction in comparative terms. According to the text, while the ΛCDM model would interpret greater mass as associated with greater gravitational redshift, DUT predicts the opposite: more massive galaxies, at the same distance, should exhibit lower average redshift, since more intense gravitational contraction would reduce the wavelength of emitted photons. The article presents this hypothesis as a potentially falsifiable test. The same work presents a table of 50 galaxy pairs, with approximate distance, estimated mass, and redshift, and states that 47 of the 50 pairs satisfy the condition predicted by DUT: at the same distance, the more massive galaxy would exhibit lower redshift. The text interprets this result as favorable to DUT and contrary to ΛCDM. From a methodological standpoint, however, this result calls for caution. The article states that the data were obtained by querying an artificial intelligence tool, and the excerpt analyzed does not provide a detailed description of an astronomical catalog, independent selection criteria, mass uncertainties, distance uncertainties, or statistical error treatment. For a specialized academic community, these points would be decisive. The prediction is clear and therefore interesting; but its validation would require reconstructing the data from verifiable astronomical catalogs, with distances obtained by methods independent of redshift, masses estimated by homogeneous criteria, and adequate statistical treatment. The galactic age hypothesis is developed in the article The Age of Galaxies as a Determining Factor for Redshift. The text starts from the idea that gravitational contraction is cumulative over time; therefore, an older galaxy would have accumulated greater contraction and should exhibit lower redshift than a younger galaxy under similar conditions. To test this hypothesis, the article uses morphology as a proxy for age: elliptical and lenticular galaxies are treated as old populations, while spiral galaxies are treated as younger populations with active star formation. The article presents average redshift differences in clusters such as Virgo, Coma, and Centaurus. In all the cases listed, elliptical/lenticular galaxies appear with lower average redshift than spiral galaxies. The text interprets this pattern as aligned with DUT's qualitative prediction. However, the document itself acknowledges that the ΛCDM model offers a widely accepted alternative explanation: the difference could result from morphological segregation, peculiar velocities, and cluster dynamics. Elliptical galaxies, being older and more relaxed, would tend to occupy more central regions, while spirals could be in wider orbits or in the process of infall into the cluster, producing a higher average Doppler redshift. This acknowledgment is methodologically important. It shows that the same observation can be compatible with two different interpretations. For DUT's interpretation to prevail, it would be necessary to demonstrate that the temporal contraction effect dominates over orbital dynamics. The article itself states, further on, that DUT's interpretation would require a more robust mathematical formalization of the relationship between lifetime, contraction, and redshift, capable of overcoming the established dynamical explanation. The most methodologically refined study in the corpus is the article on mass-redshift correlation in subgroups with controlled radial velocities. In it, the sample is restricted to 20 elliptical/lenticular galaxies from the Coma Cluster, seeking to control for distance and stellar age. The set is then divided into subgroups with a redshift variation of approximately 150 km/s, aiming to reduce the noise produced by peculiar velocities. DUT's prediction is a negative correlation between stellar mass and observed redshift. The results are mixed. Two subgroups show a negative correlation — one with a strong correlation of −0.8477 and another with a moderate correlation of −0.6789 — while another shows a perfect positive correlation, but with only two galaxies, and another shows a weak positive correlation. The article itself interprets the results as partially favorable to DUT but acknowledges that the absence of a universal negative correlation indicates that the proposed effect would not be the only factor at play. The value of this study lies less in offering a definitive demonstration and more in proposing a variable-isolation strategy. By controlling for morphology, distance, and radial velocity, the article attempts to reduce factors that could mask the predicted correlation. At the same time, its limitations are made explicit: small subgroup size, the use of stellar mass as an imperfect approximation of total mass, possible projection effects, and differences in depth within the cluster. The text concludes that the negative correlation appears in controlled subgroups, but that the mass-dependent contraction mechanism still requires further investigation and mathematical formalization. This observational section is the most important for the future academic evaluation of DUT. It shows that the theory can generate testable predictions, but it also makes clear that its current results are still preliminary. The theory would advance considerably if its tests were replicated using public catalogs, transparent criteria, larger samples, uncertainty analysis, Bayesian or frequentist comparison with alternative models, and ex ante quantitative prediction. 6. Discussion Taken together, the documents present DUT as a program of conceptual unification. Its ambition is to replace multiple hypotheses or entities — dark energy, dark matter, accelerated expansion, and certain effects attributed to local dynamics — with a single physical hypothesis: the continuous gravitational contraction of space and of measurement standards. This ambition appears clearly in both the cosmological articles and the articles on galactic dynamics. The article on the dark matter effect, for example, states that the model would provide a unified framework for apparent cosmic acceleration and for anomalous rotation curves. DUT's main virtue, in the corpus analyzed, is its attempt to formulate falsifiable predictions. The “greater mass, lower redshift” relation, the “greater age, lower redshift” hypothesis, and the tests on subgroups with controlled radial velocity are examples of propositions that can, in principle, be submitted to empirical evaluation. This is a positive aspect of the program, as it shifts it from a purely speculative formulation to a field in which its results can be confronted with observational data. The main weakness lies in the transition from a qualitative hypothesis to a fully specified quantitative model. DUT states that contraction depends on the intensity of the gravitational field, mass, and exposure time, but the documents still leave gaps in the general formalization of this dependency. The introduction of auxiliary hypotheses, such as the inverse proportionality between contraction time and gravitational field intensity, allows the derivations to proceed, but needs to be justified, tested, and integrated into a more general mathematical framework. Another problem is the need to distinguish contraction effects from effects already known in observational astrophysics. Redshift differences in clusters may arise from peculiar velocities, morphological segregation, depth within the cluster, and infall dynamics; rotation curves involve baryonic mass distribution, gas, halo, and galaxy inclination; relations such as Tully-Fisher depend on sample selection, photometric band, stellar mass, baryonic mass, and intrinsic scatter. The documents acknowledge part of these difficulties, especially in the case of morphological differences and the Coma subgroups. Therefore, the most defensible academic contribution of DUT, at this stage, is not the definitive refutation of ΛCDM, but the formulation of an alternative program that produces observational hypotheses and calls for specific tests. Appropriate scientific language should avoid absolute conclusions and favor formulations such as “the preliminary data are compatible with,” “the results suggest,” “the hypothesis requires independent verification,” or “the model proposes an alternative interpretation.” This caution brings the article closer to the evaluation standards of international journals and reduces the risk of conclusions stronger than the data allow. 7. Conclusion The analysis of the documents shows that the Decreasing Universe Theory is built around a simple and far-reaching hypothesis: gravitational fields would produce continuous contraction of space, matter, and measuring instruments. From this premise, the texts seek to reinterpret phenomena across different scales: cosmological redshift, apparent distance, time dilation in Type Ia supernovae, the wavelength of the CMB, galactic rotation curves, the Tully-Fisher relation, and correlations between mass, age, and redshift. The program shows internal coherence insofar as it applies the same hypothesis to various problems. Its strength lies in its unifying ambition and in the formulation of potentially falsifiable predictions. The articles on mass-redshift, age-redshift, and Coma subgroups indicate an effort to make the theory empirically testable, which is an indispensable aspect of any alternative cosmological proposal. However, the documents also reveal significant limitations. DUT still needs a more complete mathematical formulation of the relationship between contraction, mass, gravitational field, time, and photon emission/propagation. It also needs to be tested against independent astronomical catalogs, larger samples, rigorous control of dynamical variables, and quantitative comparison with competing models. The evidence presented is suggestive within the theory's own interpretive framework, but is not yet sufficient to establish a refutation of ΛCDM or a replacement of the prevailing cosmological paradigm. Thus, the most balanced conclusion is that DUT should be understood as an alternative theoretical program under development. Its academic relevance will depend on its ability to convert its hypotheses into precise, replicable quantitative predictions capable of unambiguously distinguishing gravitational contraction effects from already-known dynamical effects. The most promising path forward involves building more robust mathematical models, reanalyzing large observational databases, and publishing independent tests that allow the assessment, under more rigorous statistical and physical criteria, of whether the patterns predicted by DUT persist when subjected to the scrutiny of the scientific community. Shorts/Gemini_DUT_Eng.pdf: Gemini: The Decreasing Universe Theory (DUT) Deep Dive: The Decreasing Universe Theory (DUT) The Decreasing Universe Theory is a non-standard cosmological model that suggests the observed expansion of the universe is a perceptual illusion caused by the continuous contraction of all matter and the units of measurement we use to define "size." 1. The Geometry of "Shrinkage" In the standard $LCDM$ (Lambda Cold Dark Matter) model, the metric of the universe is defined by a scale factor $a(t)$ that increases over time. In the DUT, the scale factor of the universe remains constant ($a = 1$), but the characteristic lengths of all material objects—such as the Bohr radius of an atom—are decreasing. • Proportionality: If every atom in the universe shrinks by $50\%$, and your ruler also shrinks by $50\%$, you cannot measure the change locally. However, when you look at light coming from a distant galaxy (light that was emitted when atoms were still "large"), that light appears to have a longer wavelength relative to your now-smaller sensors. • The "Static" Container: The universe is viewed as a geometric container that does not change. There is no "edge" expanding into nothingness; rather, we are "receding" into the micro-scale. 2. The Role of Physical Constants For this theory to work without breaking the laws of physics in our daily lives, several fundamental constants must be "covariant" (they must change together). Proponents like Vesselin Noninski suggest that: • Mass and Energy: If the size of a particle decreases, its energy density must change. Some versions of the theory suggest that the mass of particles is actually increasing as they shrink to conserve angular momentum. • The Speed of Light ($c$): In a shrinking universe, the speed of light might appear constant to us, but in an absolute sense, it would need to scale with the shrinking atoms to ensure that the time it takes for an electron to orbit a nucleus remains consistent with our perception of time. 3. Solving the "Dark" Mysteries The most attractive part of the DUT is how it handles the "missing" pieces of modern physics: • No Dark Energy: In the Big Bang model, we need Dark Energy to explain why the expansion is accelerating. In the DUT, "acceleration" is simply the rate at which our atoms are shrinking. If the shrinkage is exponential, the "expansion" looks accelerated to us. • No Big Bang Singularity: If the universe's "container" is static and eternal, we avoid the mathematical nightmare of $t=0$, where density becomes infinite. The universe didn't start as a point; it’s just that "we" used to be much larger. 4. Historical Context: Who supports this? While it is not a "mainstream" theory, it echoes ideas from several 20th-century physicists: • Vesselin Noninski: Perhaps the most vocal modern proponent, he has published papers arguing that the Redshift is a purely terrestrial/local phenomenon of changing atomic scales. • Paul Dirac (Large Numbers Hypothesis): While not exactly the same, Dirac famously toyed with the idea that fundamental constants (like gravity) change over time. • Fred Hoyle (Steady State Theory): DUT shares a "spirit" with the Steady State theory, which disliked the idea of a "beginning" to time and preferred a universe that looks the same throughout eternity. Was there a Not necessarily; the universe Yes, 13.8 billion years ago. beginning? could be eternal. What is the Redshift? A Doppler-like effect of A "fossil" signal from larger Question Big Bang Answer Decreasing Universe Answer stretching space. atoms of the past. Heat death or "The Big We continue to shrink into the What is the future? Rip." sub-microscopic. This theory essentially suggests we are living in a cosmic hall of mirrors where our own changing size dictates our reality. 1. The Core Concept: Shrinkage instead of Expansion The basic premise is that space itself isn't growing. Instead, matter (atoms, planets, stars, and ourselves) is decreasing in size. • The Illusion of Expansion: If everything we use to measure the universe— including our rulers and our own bodies—is shrinking at a constant rate, it creates the optical illusion that the distances between galaxies are increasing. • The Room Analogy: Imagine you are in a 5-meter room. If you and your measuring tape shrink by half, when you measure the room again, it will appear to be 10 meters long. The room didn't change; you just got smaller. 2. Explaining "Redshift" In standard cosmology, redshift occurs because light waves are "stretched" as they travel through expanding space. In the Decreasing Universe theory, redshift would happen because: 1. Atoms in the past were larger. 2. Larger atoms emit light with longer (redder) wavelengths. 3. When that "ancient" light reaches us today (now that we are smaller and emit shorter, bluer light), it appears shifted toward the red spectrum compared to our current standards. Comparative Summary Feature Standard Model (Big Bang) Decreasing Universe Space Expanding. Static or stays the same. Matter Constant size. Shrinking. Redshift Light stretched by space. Light emitted by larger atoms. Status Proven by multiple evidences. Fringe/theoretical hypothesis. It’s a concept that often pops up in alternative physics forums or discussions about the relativity of measurement, but so far, the universe seems to be "winning" the expansion race in a very literal way.
